does: This paper introduces Persona Attack, a novel jailbreak technique that exploits large language models' conversational memory by incrementally injecting instructions across multiple turns rather than relying on single-prompt attacks. The method systematically accumulates adversarial instructions in the model's context window, causing the model to progressively deprioritize its safety training in favor of the injected personas and guidelines. Through experiments on multiple widely-used LLMs, the authors demonstrate that attack success rates increase as more instructions accumulate, reaching up to 95% under optimal instruction combinations, revealing that memory-based vulnerabilities represent a distinct and potent attack surface distinct from traditional prompt injection.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of jailbreak research that has exposed vulnerabilities in LLM safety training, moving beyond earlier single-prompt injection techniques that attempt to override guardrails in one exchange. It extends prior work on adversarial prompting and prompt injection by recognizing and exploiting a dimension largely overlooked by previous research: the temporal and cumulative nature of conversational context. The paper responds to the assumption underlying many safety approaches that individual prompts can be evaluated in isolation, and connects to broader research on how LLMs process instruction hierarchies and maintain instruction fidelity across longer conversations.
enables: This research opens important directions for both attack and defense. It highlights the need for safety mechanisms that account for cumulative instruction effects across conversation turns, suggesting future work should examine how models weight conflicting instructions temporally. The findings motivate development of memory-aware alignment techniques and context-based safety checks that monitor instruction drift throughout multi-turn interactions. Additionally, this work enables more sophisticated threat modeling that considers realistic attack scenarios where adversaries have extended interaction time, and points toward the evaluation of LLM robustness not just at individual prompts but across the full trajectory of a conversation.
